explanation: This property enforces that for every time a HELLO message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node ('hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node'), it must be preceded (without any possibility to send HELLO prior to the sequence) by queries from the Local Node to the Link Set, Neighbor Set, and MPR Set, in that strict order. Any violation of this chain before sending HELLO constitutes an error. The chosen template (index 8) expresses: 'globally, whenever a HELLO is sent, it must be the case that, up to that point, it was always the case that before, the required queries were made.' Since LTL itself cannot enforce strict order over arbitrary-length gaps directly, the conjunction ensures that sending a HELLO is only allowed if the preconditions (all three queries) hold, thus capturing the described safety property for every DM_i.

The property requires that, for every DM_i, an error occurs if a HELLO message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node ('hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node') and this is not preceded by the Local Node querying the Link Set ('local_node_queried_link_set'), the Neighbor Set ('local_node_queried_neighbor_set'), and the MPR Set ('local_node_queried_mpr_set'). To model this, we specify that globally, if a HELLO message is sent, then globally prior to that (in the unbounded past) it must not be the case that this HELLO message is sent without the sequence of queries occurring before. This is captured by stating: whenever 'hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node' occurs, it must always be preceded by (in any order) 'local_node_queried_link_set', 'local_node_queried_neighbor_set', and 'local_node_queried_mpr_set' occurring since the last HELLO message. In LTL, we encode the violation as the absence of all three queries at the time a HELLO message is sent, and universally forbid such an error globally.

We use template 8, encoding that whenever a HELLO message is sent, it must always (globally) be accompanied by these queries before the event (negation of the error condition). The error condition ('bool_exp3') is 'hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node & (!local_node_queried_link_set | !local_node_queried_neighbor_set | !local_node_queried_mpr_set)', so 'G[0,] !bool_exp3'.

This LTL property formalizes the requirement that every time an HELLO message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node, it must be the case that the Local Node has previously queried the Link Set, Neighbor Set, and MPR Set components. If this sequence is not followed (i.e., if a HELLO message is sent without these queries occurring before), it constitutes an error. We express this by stating: globally, whenever a HELLO message is sent, it must be true that at some point prior, the Local Node has queried all three required sets. This is captured by the implication: if a HELLO message is sent, then in the past (in any execution), the Local Node has performed all three queries. In LTL, since the typical (PLTL) logic doesn't have explicit 'past' operators, we can equivalently encode this by forbidding the sending of the HELLO message unless all three queries have just occurred, i.e., it is globally forbidden to send a HELLO message if the sequence has not been completed. This is implemented by template 8: 'G (bool_exp1 -> G[0,] !bool_exp3)', where bool_exp1 represents the universality for every DM_i (which can be left as TRUE here), and bool_exp3 captures the sending of the HELLO message without the required chain.

This LTL formula enforces that for all DM_i (universality over DM_i is captured in the global 'G'), whenever a HELLO message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node (hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node), it is always the case previously that the Local Node queried the Link Set, Neighbor Set, and MPR Set in sequence; otherwise, an error occurs. The formula is encoded such that globally (G), if a HELLO is sent, then globally in the past up to that point, the required queries must have happened; otherwise, it is considered an error. Here, we use an implication: if a HELLO message is sent, then globally it's never the case that the Local Node has not queried all three sets. This prevents HELLO messages from being sent unless the three queries have already happened. We encode the error as the negation of the conjunction of these three queries. The negation (error) must never occur when a HELLO is sent. The pattern is: 'G(bool_exp1 -> G[0,] !bool_exp3)', where bool_exp1 identifies a HELLO message, and bool_exp3 is the error condition.

The LTL property ensures that every time a HELLO message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node, it is always required that, globally from that point, none of the required pre-queries (queries to the Link Set, Neighbor Set, and MPR Set) are ever missing. In this context, the negation of the correct query sequence (i.e., that a HELLO message is sent without the Local Node having just queried all three sets) would be considered an error, which must never happen. Here, bool_exp1 expresses the correct conditional, and bool_exp3 is set to the improper event: that a HELLO message is sent without the sequence. By wrapping this in a global scope, we forbid this improper situation everywhere. This LTL restricts system traces such that the undesired chain (sending a HELLO without all three queries) never occurs, for all DM_i.

This property ensures that for all possible DMs (the quantification is outside the formula, so here we express it for a fixed DM_i context), it is globally the case that whenever an HELLO message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node, it must have been necessarily preceded (at some time in the past or immediately before) by the querying of the Link Set, Neighbor Set, and MPR Set by the Local Node. If this chain is violated (i.e., any HELLO message is sent without this sequence of queries), that is considered an error. This chain of required operations prior to the message being sent is captured by blocking (forbidding) the occurrence of a HELLO message unless all three queries happened before or simultaneously. Thus, we use the template G (bool_exp1 -> G[0,] !bool_exp3) with bool_exp1 expressing that a HELLO message is sent and bool_exp3 being the negation of the logical AND of all required queries, i.e., if any was not performed, then the error occurs.

The property requires that in every execution (for every DM_i), whenever an HELLO message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node (hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node), the Local Node must have previously queried the Link Set, Neighbor Set, and MPR Set components (i.e., local_node_queried_link_set, local_node_queried_neighbor_set, local_node_queried_mpr_set). An error occurs if an HELLO message is issued without this chain of operations, so we encode that globally, if an HELLO message is sent, then in the past (in the same or earlier state), all three queries must have occurred. In LTL, this is encoded by insisting that globally, if an HELLO message is sent, then the three queries must all be true now or have already been true, i.e., 'if hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node then all previous queries must have occured'. For safety, we enforce that it is always forbidden for an HELLO message to be sent unless all three conditions are satisfied. Thus, this is a global restriction template.

This LTL formula expresses that, for every DM_i (i.e., always), if an HELLO message is sent from the DM_i to the Local Node, then, before that, the Local Node must have queried the Link Set, Neighbor Set, and MPR Set components. If these queries do NOT all occur prior to the HELLO message, an error is considered to have occurred (i.e., this trace is considered invalid). The formula uses a global property ensuring that whenever a HELLO message is sent (hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node), it is necessarily preceded by the querying of Link Set, Neighbor Set, and MPR Set by the Local Node. This is best captured by stating that globally, it is NOT possible for a HELLO message to be sent unless all queries have occurred previously in sequence. This is encoded as a forbidden pattern using a negated implication (whenever a HELLO message is sent, the conjunction of all three queries must be true at some point prior or simultaneously).

The property states that in every instant where an HELLO message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node ('hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node'), it must always be the case that immediately before this event, the Local Node has queried the Link Set, the Neighbor Set, and the MPR Set. This ensures that the required chain of operations (queries on all three sets) is performed before any HELLO message is exchanged, matching the text's requirement to prohibit errors arising from skipping these steps. The property is universally quantified over all DM_i due to 'every DM_i' in the requirement. Expressed as an implication: whenever 'hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node' is true, it must be that the Local Node has just performed all three required queries.

The requirement is that before any HELLO message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node, the Local Node must have performed queries to Link Set, Neighbor Set, and MPR Set components. An error (bad state) is the sending of an HELLO message without the prior queries. This must hold for every DM_i and always. Thus, for every occurrence of 'hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node', the prior queries must all have been performed. We encode the error as the condition where 'hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node' is true and at least one of the required queries (to Link Set, Neighbor Set, and MPR Set) is false. Using template 0 ('G[0,] !bool_exp3'), this expresses that it is always the case (globally) that the error state (sending HELLO without the required queries) does not occur.
